findings.description: get() calls _purge_expired() on every lookup, turning an O(1) token fetch into a full O(n) scan of the entire session dict — the read hot path degrades linearly with the number of active sessions; expiry should be lazy (check the single fetched session) or amortized, not a full sweep per read.
_purge_expired() materializes a full intermediate list of dead tokens via a comprehension over all sessions every time it runs, allocating O(n) garbage per invocation on top of the scan cost.
active_count() triggers another full O(n) _purge_expired() sweep; if polled by metrics/monitoring this repeatedly scans the whole store, and the purge could be decoupled from the count.

_new_token() builds the token with a Python-level generator calling random.choice 32 times per token; random.choices(alphabet, k=TOKEN_LENGTH) is a single C-level call and faster on the create path under high churn.
Tokens are generated with the non-cryptographic random module, making them predictable — security concern, deferring detail to critic-security.
